headline: What to Do Immediately After a Machinery Accident at a Utah Job Site
articleBody:
What to Do Immediately After a Machinery Accident at a Utah Job Site

Industrial machinery accidents are among the most traumatic events a worker can experience. Whether it is a conveyor belt incident in Salt Lake City or a forklift malfunction in an Ogden warehouse, the minutes and hours following the accident are critical for both your health and your legal rights. At Just Work Comp Law, we have seen how a few early mistakes can jeopardize an entire claim. Here is exactly what you need to do.

1. Seek Immediate Medical Attention

Your health is the absolute priority. In a machinery accident, internal injuries or "crush syndrome" may not be immediately apparent. Go to the nearest emergency room or industrial clinic. Tell the doctor exactly how the injury happened and that it occurred while you were working. This creates the "medical causation" link that insurance companies often try to dispute later.

2. Report the Injury in Writing

While you might tell your foreman or supervisor verbally, you must provide a written report as soon as possible. In Utah, you officially have 180 days, but waiting even a few days gives the insurance company room to argue that the accident happened elsewhere. A simple email or text message to your supervisor suffices as a paper trail.

3. Do Not Clean or Repair the Machine

This is the most common pitfall. Employers often want to fix the machine and get back to work immediately. However, the broken machine is your primary evidence. If guards were missing or sensors failed, that evidence must be preserved. If possible, take photos of the machine exactly as it sat at the time of the injury. If the machine is repaired before it is inspected, you may lose the ability to sue the manufacturer for a defect.

4. Identify Witnesses

In a loud factory or busy construction site, coworkers might be the only people who saw what actually happened. Get their names and phone numbers. The insurance company will conduct their own "investigation" and may pressure employees to change their stories; having your own list of witnesses protects the truth.

5. Be Cautious with Insurance Adjusters

Within days, you will likely receive a call from a workers' compensation insurance adjuster. They may sound friendly, but their goal is to minimize the company's financial exposure. Do not allow them to record a statement until you have consulted with an attorney. They are trained to ask "trap" questions that can make it look like the accident was solely your fault.

6. Contact a Workers' Compensation Specialist

Machinery accidents often involve complex questions of liability. Was the machine defective? Did a third-party maintenance crew fail to service it? Just Work Comp Law specializes in these specific types of industrial accidents across Utah and Idaho. We ensure that evidence is preserved and that you receive the maximum benefits allowed by law. If you have been hurt by machinery, call Just Work Comp Law today at 801-746-0290 for a free, no-pressure consultation. We focus on your recovery while we handle the legal battle.
